content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 22, 1994

Michael Lyions
Law Offices of Rushing,
Lyions & Anderson
125 McHenry Avenue
Modesto, CA 95354-0541

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-013

Dear Mr. Lyions:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of John B. Anderson, a City of Ceres Planning Commissioner,
regarding his responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. May Planning Commissioner Anderson appear before the
General Plan Review Committee for the purpose of advocating the
interest of his clients regarding possible amendments to the Ceres
General Plan?

2. May Planning Commissioner Anderson participate in any
discussions, recommendations or decisions of the planning
commission when the General Plan Review Committee makes its
periodic reports to the planning commission?

3. If it is determined that Commissioner Anderson has a
financial interest in decisions of the planning commission
regarding amendment of the General Plan, thereby precluding his
participation in such decisions, is it possible to bifurcate these
decisions from decisions that would not have a material financial
effect on his clients' properties, so that the commissioner can
participate in other aspects of the General Plan update?

4. May Commissioner Anderson appear before the city council
to advocate the position of his clients when the General Plan
Review Committee makes its periodic reports to the city council?

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-013
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

     1.     Commissioner Anderson may not appear before the General
Plan Review Committee.

     2.     Commissioner Anderson may not appear before his own
agency (the planning commission) or make any contact with the city
staff regarding a matter which is, or may come, before the
planning commission.  Such appearances or contacts before the
planning commission would be an improper attempt to use his
official position to influence a decision.

     3.     We have advised that large, complex decisions under
certain circumstances may be divided into separate decisions when
an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the
decision which is not interdependent upon other components.  The
official may then participate as to the other components in which
he has no financial interest.  However, under the facts presented
in your letter, it may be that the decisions are too interrelated
to be considered separately.  We do not have sufficient
information to make this determination.

     4.     Commissioner Anderson may not appear before the city
council on behalf of his clients to influence the recommendations
of the city council regarding the amendments to the General Plan
as long as this matter is within or before his agency.  This means
that as long as this matter is, or may come, before the planning
commission, or is appealable to, and may be reconsidered by, the
planning commission, Commissioner Anderson may not appear before
either the General Plan Review Committee or the city council to
influence their decisions.  However, once the final form and
content of the amendment to the General Plan has been voted upon
by the planning commission, Commissioner Anderson may appear
before the city council on behalf of his clients provided that he
states on the record he is not appearing in his official capacity.

FACTS

     John B. Anderson is a Planning Commissioner for the City of
Ceres.  Members of the planning commission are appointed by the
mayor with the approval of the city council.

     Pursuant to the terms of its municipal code, the City of
Ceres has created a General Plan Review Committee.  The General
Plan Review Committee is currently composed of two planning
commissioners, two city councilmembers and one member of the parks
and recreation commission.  Planning Commissioner Anderson is not
a member of this committee.

     The city is presently in the process of making a
comprehensive amendment and update of all elements of its General
Plan.  The city has retained a consultant to assist in this
process, and it is contemplated that the final adoption of the new
General Plan will take approximately two years.  The General Plan

Our File No. A-94-013
Page 3


Review Committee will be holding periodic meetings which will be
open to the public. During such meetings the General Plan Review
Committee will be working with city staff, the city's consultant,
and other interested citizens and groups for the purpose of
reviewing, discussing, and making recommendations to both the
Ceres Planning Commission and the Ceres City Council regarding the
form and content of the revised General Plan for the city.

It is further contemplated that the General Plan Review
Committee will from time to time make interim recommendations to
both the Ceres Planning Commission and the Ceres City Council
regarding certain elements or aspects of the General Plan and
request periodic guidance and direction from the planning
commission and the city council regarding its activities. The
General Plan Review Committee will ultimately make a final report
and recommendation to the planning commission and the city council
regarding the final form and content of the city's revised General
Plan.

Planning Commissioner Anderson is engaged in the business of
land use planning and real estate consulting. He has been
retained by clients who seek his services regarding the
development of their property. His clients own approximately
eighty (80) acres of undeveloped property which lies outside the
present city limits but within the sphere of influence of the City
of Ceres and will be affected by decisions made regarding the
General Plan. In addition, during the period of time that the
city is considering the adoption of its General Plan update,
Commissioner Anderson may be retained by other clients who engage
his services regarding the development of properties that may be
affected by the amendment of the General Plan.

Planning Commissioner Anderson wishes to appear at the
meetings of the General Plan Review Committee to represent the
interests of his clients in an effort to secure a favorable
recommendation from the committee regarding General Plan
amendments which affect his clients' properties and which will
enhance the development possibilities of their properties. When
making appearances at the meetings of the General Plan Review
Committee, Commissioner Anderson would not be appearing in his
official capacity as a planning commissioner, but in his capacity
as a land use planner and real estate consultant and only on
behalf of individual clients who have retained him for that
purpose. Compensation from his clients will exceed $250.00 during
a twelve month period.

ANALYSIS

The Act was adopted by the voters of California by initiative
in 1974. The purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act was to ensure that public officials, whether elected or
appointed, would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)

Our File No. A-94-013
Page 4


     In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 prohibits a public
official from making, participating in making of or in any way
attempting to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. An official has a financial
interest in a decision that will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect,different from the effect on the general public,
on the official or on the official's immediate family or on the
following:

          (a) Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                         * * *

          (c) Any source of income, other than gifts
     and other than loans by a commercial lending
     institution in the regular course of business on
     terms available to the public without regard to
     official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
     received by or promised to the public official
     within 12 months prior to the time when the
     decision is made.

          (d) Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.

                    Section 87103(a), (c), and (d).

     Commissioner Anderson is a public official. (Section 82048.)
Therefore, he is disqualified from making or participating in
making any decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on his business, on any business in
which he is a director, officer, or partner, or on a client of his
business who has paid or promised to pay him $250 or more within
12 months before a governmental decision. (Section 87100; Section
87103; Regulation 18700.) Disqualification also means the
commissioner may not in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a decision in which he has a financial
interest. (Regulation 18700.1; Bowler Advice Letter, No. I-93-
287; West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413.)

     Regulation 18700.1 describes the prohibited conduct of
"attempting to use [one's] official position to influence a
decision" as follows:

          (a) With regard to a governmental decision
     which is within or before an official's agency or
     an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
     control of his or her agency, the official is
     attempting to use his or her official position to

Our File No. A-94-013
Page 5


     influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.

                         * * *

     (c) With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an agency not covered by
subsection (a), the official is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer, employee or
consultant of an agency. Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery.

     (Regulation 18700.1(a) and (c); emphasis added.)

     Pursuant to subdivision (a), Commissioner Anderson may not
appear before his own agency² (the planning commission) or make
any contact with the city staff regarding a matter which is, or
may come, before the planning commission. Such appearances or
contacts before the planning commission would be an improper
attempt to use his official position to influence a decision.
This would include any word or deed to attempt to affect or
further the agency's decision. (Collins Advice Letter,
No. 78-06-074.)

     Furthermore, Commissioner Anderson may not appear before the
General Plan Review Committee which does not appear to be a
separate local governmental agency, (see, footnote 3) but rather a
joint subcommittee composed of several city agencies, including in
pertinent part, the planning commission and the city council.
Since Commissioner Anderson cannot appear before his own agency,
the planning commission, he also cannot appear before members of
the planning commission who are serving on a subcommittee of the
planning commission.

_______
2      Section 82041 defines "local government agency" as a county,
city, or district of any kind including "any department, division,
bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing." Generally, the Commission has treated different
governmental departments as different agencies. For example, a
planning commission is a distinct agency from the city council or
the department of city planning. (Stout Advice Letter,
No. I-88-313.)

Our File No. A-94-013
Page 6


     Finally, under the facts presented, Commissioner Anderson may
not appear before the city council on behalf of his clients to
influence the recommendations of the city council regarding the
amendments to the General Plan as long as this matter is within or
before the planning commission.  Under your facts, the same city
officials and staff are reviewing the possible amendments to the
General Plan and the elements thereof. Both the planning
commission and the city council review the interim recommendations
made by the General Plan Review Committee and then pass them back
to that committee. Because the same city officials and staff are
reviewing all the elements and aspects of the amendments to the
General Plan update, Commissioner Anderson is prohibited from
attempting to influence them about a decision that the planning
commission eventually will consider and vote upon.

     As long as a governmental decision is within or before an
official's agency, the official may not attempt to use his or her
official position to influence that decision. (Regulation
18700.1(a).) Therefore, Commissioner Anderson may not appear
before either the General Plan Review Committee or the city
council to influence a matter which is, or may come, before the
planning commission, or which is appealable to, and may be
reconsidered by, the planning commission. (Bowler Advice Letter,
supra.)

     However, once the final form and content of the amendment to
the General Plan has been voted upon by the planning commission,
Commissioner Anderson may appear before the city council on behalf
of his clients provided that he does not act in his official
capacity or purport to act on behalf of the planning commission.
(Regulation 18700.1(c).)

Bifurcation

     You have also asked if it is possible to separate
discussions, advice, direction, and comments regarding the update
of the General Plan, in other words, segregate a decision in which
the councilmember has a financial interest from other decisions, so
that Commissioner Anderson may then participate as to the other
components of the General Plan in which he has no financial
interest. We have advised that large, complex decisions under
certain circumstances may be divided into separate decisions when
an official has a disqualifying interest in one component of the
decision which is not interdependent upon other components. The
official may then participate as to the other components in which
he has no financial interest. (Merkuloff Advice Letter, No. I-90-
542; Killian Advice Letter, No. A-89-522; and Huffaker Advice
Letter, No. A-86-343.)

Our File No. A-94-013
Page 7


Thus, Commissioner Anderson may participate in a governmental
decision in which he has no financial interest if the procedure
outlined below is adhered to:

(1) The decision in which the commissioner has a
disqualifying financial interest is segregated from the other
decisions.

(2) The decision in which the commissioner has a financial
interest is considered first, and a final decision is reached by
the planning commission without the participation of the
commissioner. This decision must be final and not subject to
change by other subsequent decisions regarding the proposed
amendment to the General Plan.

(3) Once a final decision has been reached on the matter
that has a material financial effect on the commissioner's
business or on any of his clients, the commissioner may
participate in the deliberations and vote regarding the other
components of the General Plan, so long as these deliberations and
votes do not affect the previous decision or decisions from which
he was disqualified. (Lanzone Advice Letter, No. A-93-313;
Merkuloff supra; and McNatt Advice Letter, No. I-90-714.)

Please note that a series of decisions may be too
interrelated to be considered separately. (Miller Advice Letter,
No. A-82-119.) For example, in a decision to select one of two
autopark sites, a decision to select one of the sites is
essentially a decision against the other autopark site. (See e.g.
Boogaard Advice Letter, No. I-90-347; Miranda Advice Letter, No.
I-88-373; Miranda Advice Letter, No. I-88-253.) Similarly,
decisions regarding the bike or pedestrian paths, the proposed
freeway improvements, or any other aspect of the circulation
element of a general plan may be linked to decisions which might
affect the road expansion or road median. If such is the case,
the decisions may not be bifurcated as one decision will have an
effect on the other related decisions.

In your letter you state that the city is taking a
comprehensive approach to the General Plan update and that all
components to the amendment are interrelated. Thus, it may be
that the decisions are too interrelated to be considered
separately as required in the procedure set forth above. We do
not have sufficient information to make this determination.

Our File No. A-94-013
Page 8


If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.³

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel




3 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
